Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Person Most Knowledgeable Pursuant To Ccp 2025.480
Off Calendar Sep-28-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance by any party or counsel.

There being no appearances, the tentative ruling on PLAINTIFF RYAN KIME'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Person Most Knowledgeable Pursuant To CCP 2025.480 is adopted as follows:

The motion of Ryan J. Kime M.D. is DENIED.

"[A]ny party may obtain discovery regarding any matter, not privileged, that is relevant to the subject matter involved in the pending action or to the determination of any motion made in that action, if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code Sec. 2017.010.

That said, the right to conduct discovery against a non-party is necessarily more limited. "[W]hen dealing with an entity which is not . . . a party to the litigation, the court should attempt to structure discovery in a manner which is least burdensome to such an entity." Calcor Space Facility, Inc. v. Superior Court (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 216, 222. In that case, the Court "must carefully weigh the cost, time, expense and disruption of normal business resulting from an order compelling the discovery against the probative value of the material which might be disclosed if the discovery is ordered." Id. at 223.

The motion requests an order compelling testimony from Medical Staff of Mercy Hospital ("Mercy"), which is not a party to the litigation, on three topics identified as (#16, 17 and 19). The probative value of the discovery at issue is vigorously disputed by the parties. However, the court need not resolve the motion on the basis of whether the information sought
is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. Based on the facts and arguments presented in the moving and opposing papers, defendant's awareness of the information sought -- and what it did or did not do with that knowledge -- appears more germane to the subject dispute than that of Mercy. Accordingly, it follows that the burden associated with producing the requisite information is more fairly and appropriately borne by the defendant rather than by non-party Mercy.

The request of Dr. Kime and Mercy for monetary sanctions is DENIED.

The prevailing party is prepare a form of order in compliance with CRC 3.1312 and submit it to Judge Pro Tem Vestal at: Pvestal@nvlawllp.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Peter Vestal. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Matter Not Reported. (302/JPT)
